region_of_origin: Indian subcontinent; served in Turkish-Lebanese restaurant context in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Malai Tikka exists; closest_parent 'chicken kebab food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific Malai Tikka entry; 'Chicken meat, grilled' (A0CKN) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for Malai Tikka as a specific dish; none assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Malai Tikka consists of approximately 200 g of grilled chicken pieces (3–4 skewers), served with mint chutney and sliced onions.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken tikka, grilled — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Malai Tikka is a cream-marinated variant of chicken tikka. ICMR-NIN values for grilled chicken tikka (per 100 g edible portion) are used as the primary base. Macros are adjusted upward for fat and energy to account for the heavy cream (malai) and yogurt marinade characteristic of Malai Tikka, consistent with published recipe analyses. USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, NDB 05064 — Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, cooked, roasted) was used as a secondary cross-reference for protein and energy plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Malai Tikka. GI estimated at 30 (low) by analogy with grilled marinated chicken dishes and high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat preparations documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care). The very low carbohydrate content (2.8 g per 100 g) means glycemic impact is negligible regardless of GI. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (30 × 5.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.7, confirming low glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~840 mg per typical 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful that a 200 g serving of Malai Tikka contributes approximately 840 mg sodium (~37% of the 2300 mg daily limit), and should limit portion size or request reduced-salt preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.8 g per 100 g) and high protein (22.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Malai Tikka is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic load (~1.7 per serving), though the cream-based marinade adds saturated fat that should be accounted for in overall dietary fat targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~5.5 g per 100 g) from heavy cream marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption of Malai Tikka as the heavy cream marinade contributes approximately 11 g of saturated fat per 200 g serving, which may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels when consumed frequently.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Malai Tikka is a popular Indian grilled chicken dish in which boneless chicken pieces are marinated in a rich mixture of heavy cream (malai), yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, green chilli, white pepper, cardamom, and lemon juice before being cooked in a tandoor (clay oven) or on a grill. The name 'malai' refers to the thick cream used in the marinade, which gives the dish its characteristic mild, creamy, and subtly spiced flavour profile — distinctly less fiery than red-marinated tikka variants. The texture is tender and juicy, with a slight char from the high-heat cooking. Malai Tikka is served in Turkish and Lebanese restaurants in Singapore (notably Sultan Palace, Boat Quay) as part of a broader Middle Eastern and South Asian mixed grill menu, reflecting the culinary overlap between Indian tandoor traditions and Turkish kebab culture. Nutritionally, Malai Tikka is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish (~22.5 g protein and ~2.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g), making it suitable for low-glycaemic dietary patterns. The cream-based marinade elevates fat content (~13 g per 100 g, of which ~5.5 g is saturated), which is a consideration for cardiovascular health. Sodium content is moderate (~420 mg per 100 g) due to seasoning. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal when prepared with compliant ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Malai Tikka?
Is Malai Tikka good for diabetics?
What is the difference between Malai Tikka and regular chicken tikka?
How much protein is in Malai Tikka?
What is malai in Malai Tikka?
Where can I get Malai Tikka in Singapore?

confidence_reason: Malai Tikka as a specific dish is not directly listed in ICMR-NIN or USDA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN grilled chicken tikka data with upward adjustment for fat and energy to account for the cream-heavy marinade. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation is methodologically sound and cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy chicken data.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN (2017) grilled chicken tikka per 100 g edible portion, with fat and energy adjusted upward to reflect the heavy cream (malai) marinade. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05064) used as secondary cross-reference. 2. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008); no direct GI measurement for Malai Tikka exists in the literature. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 5. The cuisine tag 'Turkish' in the input reflects the restaurant context (Sultan Palace, Boat Quay); the dish itself is of Indian origin and is classified accordingly. 6. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant's Turkish-Lebanese cuisine context; formal halal certification should be verified with the restaurant. 7. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy chicken breast cooked data proportionally adjusted for the cream marinade fat contribution.
